# Build Specification
## AI-Assisted Artifact Operating System
## Version 1.4

---

# 1. Technical Summary

This document defines the build structure, artifact contracts, lifecycle mechanics, validation rules, and acceptance gates for the Harness.

The Harness is a governing instrument for AI-assisted project work. When applied to a host project, it deposits a `.harness/` knowledge cartridge containing the active project memory, product intent, and build mechanics. `HARNESS.md` lives at the project root as the governing protocol.

The cartridge always contains `01_PROJECT.md` as its session anchor. A `README.md` is recommended as an index of whatever else the cartridge holds. Everything beyond that is composed by the operator from available templates or created as the project requires.

---

# 2. Build Purpose

This file defines how the artifact system must be structured, validated, maintained, extended, and restarted.

It covers: canonical file map and deployment topology, artifact responsibility contracts, authority rules, lifecycle mechanics, update mechanics, editing discipline, changelog mechanics, optional artifact extension policy, migration mechanics, validation rules, restart mechanics, drift handling, and acceptance gates.

Product rationale belongs in `.harness/02_PRD.md`. Current project state belongs in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`. Reusable collaboration protocol belongs in `HARNESS.md`.

---

# 3. Runtime and Platform Assumptions

## 3.1 Format assumptions

- Artifacts are plain markdown files with two-digit numeric prefixes
- Files are readable without custom tooling and can be versioned or stored in a repository
- No database or custom application is required for the MVP

## 3.2 Collaboration assumptions

- An operator collaborates with an AI model over multiple sessions
- The AI may not retain reliable memory across sessions
- Project state must live in artifacts, not only in chat
- Artifact revisions should use targeted edits by default; full rewrites only for net-new files or significant restructures
- Substantial changes should be preceded by alignment when the scope is ambiguous

## 3.3 Tooling assumptions

The MVP does not require automated linting, schema validation, source-control hooks, document synchronization, or a web UI. The artifact system must remain useful as a markdown-only package.

---

# 4. Cartridge Structure

## 4.1 Required file

| File | Location | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `HARNESS.md` | Project root | Governing protocol — read first on every session |
| `01_PROJECT.md` | `.harness/` | Session anchor — active memory, current state, restart context |

`01_PROJECT.md` is the only enforced file in the cartridge. It is the one file a future session can always count on being present.

## 4.2 Recommended file

| File | Location | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `README.md` | `.harness/` | Cartridge index — what else is in this directory and what each file does |

`README.md` makes the cartridge navigable regardless of its shape. When the cartridge contains more than `01_PROJECT.md`, a README should be present. It does not duplicate `01_PROJECT.md` — it indexes everything else.

## 4.3 Flexible content

Everything beyond `01_PROJECT.md` and `README.md` is composed by the operator. The Harness provides templates as starting points — not requirements. A project may use one additional file or ten, structured however serves the work.

Available templates are documented in `skills/deploy.md` and `skills/adopt.md`.

## 4.4 Deployment topology

When the Harness is applied to a host project:

- `HARNESS.md` is placed at the host project root — the governing protocol the AI reads first
- The `.harness/` directory is deposited at the project root as the knowledge cartridge

The `.harness/` directory is a self-contained context deposit — a container the AI reads to understand the project. Its shape varies by project; its navigability is guaranteed by `01_PROJECT.md` and `README.md`.

## 4.5 Self-hosted instance

This repo is itself a governed instance of the Harness. The `.harness/` directory here contains the evolving context for the Harness's own development. This is the canonical demonstration of the deployment model.

---

# 5. Artifact Responsibility Contracts

## 5.1 `HARNESS.md`

Must define reusable governing protocol: governing instrument framing, deployment model, artifact roles, authority model, collaboration principles, update workflow, editing discipline, promotion and pruning, restart protocol, and skills directory policy.

Must not include project-specific scope, current project status, or unresolved project decisions.

## 5.2 `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`

Must define current project continuity: project snapshot, artifact map, current path forward, active work, recent trail, open decisions, promotion queue, do-not-resurrect notes, and restart protocol.

Must not become a duplicate of any other file, or a permanent source for stable decisions.

## 5.3 `.harness/README.md`

Must index the contents of the cartridge: one line per file, describing what it holds and why it exists. Does not duplicate `01_PROJECT.md`. Updated whenever a file is added or removed from the cartridge.

Must not hold project content — it is navigation only.

## 5.4 Additional cartridge files

Responsibility contracts for additional files are defined within each file's own Document Governance section. The operator decides what files the project needs; this Build Spec does not prescribe them beyond the enforced and recommended files above.

---

# 6. Required Local Governance Contract

Every artifact must include a `Document Governance` section defining:

1. what the file owns
2. what the file does not own
3. when to update the file
4. when not to update the file
5. what should be promoted into the file
6. what should be pruned from the file
7. conflict behavior

Keep local governance specific to the artifact. Do not copy the full global model into every file.

---

# 7. Authority Model

## 7.1 Reading order

1. `HARNESS.md` (project root)
2. `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`
3. `.harness/README.md` (if present — to understand cartridge shape and what else to read)
4. Additional cartridge files as indicated by README or current task

`.harness/01_PROJECT.md` appears early because it orients the session. `README.md` follows immediately so the AI understands the full shape of the cartridge before proceeding.

## 7.2 Authority by topic

| Topic | Authority |
|---|---|
| Reusable collaboration protocol | `HARNESS.md` |
| Current project state | `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` |
| Topic-specific durable truth | The file whose Document Governance section claims that topic |

Direct operator instruction outranks all artifacts for the current task.

## 7.3 Conflict handling rule

If two artifacts conflict, the artifact with authority for the topic controls. If a direct operator instruction changes durable truth, update the relevant artifact rather than relying on chat memory. If authority is unclear, identify the conflict before making large changes.

---

# 8. Artifact Lifecycle Model

Each artifact moves through: **draft → review → revision → stabilization → maintenance → pruning → replacement or supersession**.

**Draft**: May contain assumptions and placeholders. Must still respect artifact boundaries.

**Review**: Check role clarity, completeness, overlap, stale assumptions, conflicts with other artifacts, and missing local governance.

**Revision**: Make targeted edits by default. Use a full rewrite only when restructuring requires coordinated changes across many sections of the file.

**Stabilization**: An artifact is stable when its role is clear, required sections are present, cross-artifact conflicts are resolved, and local governance is present.

**Maintenance**: Update current state, promote stable decisions, prune stale notes, reconcile cross-artifact drift.

**Supersession**: If an artifact is replaced or renamed, identify old and new names, explain the reason, update references in related artifacts, and prevent the old artifact from remaining ambiguously authoritative.

---

# 9. Promotion Mechanics

## 9.1 Promotion sources

Most new ideas, assumptions, and unresolved decisions begin in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`.

## 9.2 Promotion destinations

| Content Type | Destination |
|---|---|
| Reusable collaboration protocol | `HARNESS.md` |
| Current project state | `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` |
| Product intent | `.harness/02_PRD.md` |
| Build mechanics | `.harness/03_BUILD.md` |

## 9.3 Promotion trigger

Promote a decision when it becomes stable enough that future work should treat it as durable truth.

## 9.4 Post-promotion pruning

After promotion, remove or compress the source note from `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`. Do not keep duplicate full-detail versions in both active memory and durable truth.

---

# 10. Pruning Mechanics

Remove anything that no longer improves future decisions. Compress history when the detail is less important than the outcome.

**From `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`**: prune stale work-in-progress notes, resolved open decisions, promoted details, completed trail items that no longer affect decisions. Keep: current phase, active work, open decisions, recent meaningful trail, promotion queue, do-not-resurrect guardrails.

**From `.harness/02_PRD.md`**: prune implementation mechanics, temporary status notes, duplicate build details, and stale future ideas.

**From `.harness/03_BUILD.md`**: prune product rationale, current phase notes, speculative ideas, and duplicate PRD language.

**From `HARNESS.md`**: prune project-specific content, domain-specific examples, obsolete artifact names, and repeated rules.

---

# 11. Artifact Update Mechanics

## 11.1 Default edit mode

Make targeted, precise edits by default. Use enough surrounding context to uniquely identify each edit target. Reserve full file rewrites for net-new files or significant structural changes where many sections require coordinated updates.

## 11.2 Alignment-first trigger

Before making large changes, align first when ambiguity exists about: target artifact, scope of change, product versus build scope, or whether the request requires restructuring versus a simple update.

## 11.3 Cross-artifact impact check

After every meaningful update, check whether another artifact is stale. A protocol change may require `HARNESS.md`; a scope change may require `.harness/02_PRD.md`; a build-contract change may require `.harness/03_BUILD.md`; a file naming change may require all artifacts.

## 11.4 Post-edit communication

After editing, communicate what changed, any assumptions made, and flag related artifacts that may need updating. Do not silently remove durable decisions when restructuring a file.

---

# 12. Changelog and Versioning Mechanics

Use simple version labels: `0.x` for early drafts, `1.0` for first stable version, minor increments for clarifications, major increments for substantial restructuring.

A separate changelog artifact is not required for MVP. Track meaningful changes in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` using recent trail entries. Create a separate changelog only if revisions become frequent, multiple collaborators are involved, or formal traceability is required.

When a trail entry is useful, capture: date or session marker, artifact changed, change summary, reason, and version if applicable.

---

# 13. Cartridge Composition

The cartridge is composed by the operator. `01_PROJECT.md` is the only required file. Everything else is added when the project warrants it.

## 13.1 Composition principle

Add a file when it reduces complexity more than it adds maintenance overhead. The test: would the project be harder to navigate or resume without this file? If yes, add it. If no, keep the information in `01_PROJECT.md`.

## 13.2 Available templates

The Harness provides templates as starting points. Templates are proposals — not requirements. Use them, adapt them, or ignore them.

Common templates:
- `product.md` — stable product intent: purpose, users, scope, non-goals, success criteria
- `project.md` — project-specific state beyond `01_PROJECT.md`: decisions log, open questions, constraints
- `build.md` — technical and build mechanics for implementation-heavy projects
- `protocol.md` — project-specific collaboration rules or domain conventions

Templates live in `skills/deploy.md` and `skills/adopt.md`.

## 13.3 Update README when adding files

Every time a file is added to or removed from `.harness/`, update `README.md` to reflect the change. This keeps the cartridge navigable.

## 13.4 Optional skills directory

A `skills/` directory may be added at the project root to hold reusable AI procedures — named, documented playbooks the AI can invoke within the project context.

Create a `skills/` directory only when reusable procedures become complex enough to justify named invocations rather than inline instruction. If created, skills are stored as individual markdown files within `skills/` and referenced from `HARNESS.md`.

---

# 14. Validation Rules

## 14.1 Package-level validation

The cartridge is valid when:

- `01_PROJECT.md` exists and is current
- `README.md` is present and accurately reflects the cartridge contents
- each file has a clear role and Document Governance section
- artifact responsibilities do not materially overlap
- current state is not confused with durable truth
- no legacy artifact remains ambiguously authoritative

## 14.2 Per-file validation

**`HARNESS.md`**: valid when it is project-agnostic, defines the governing instrument and deployment model, defines collaboration behavior and editing discipline, and does not contain project-specific details.

**`.harness/01_PROJECT.md`**: valid when it states current phase, identifies active work and open decisions, contains a promotion queue and recent trail, identifies the next recommended action, and does not duplicate content owned by other cartridge files.

**`.harness/README.md`**: valid when it lists every file in the cartridge with a one-line description of what each holds, and is current with the actual directory contents.

**Additional cartridge files**: valid when each has a Document Governance section defining what it owns and what it defers.

---

# 15. Restart and Resume Mechanics

A restart-safe project must allow a future AI session or collaborator to resume using artifacts alone.

## 15.1 Resume sequence

1. Read `HARNESS.md`
2. Read `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` — identify current phase, active work, and next action
3. Read `.harness/README.md` — understand the full cartridge shape and what else to load
4. Read additional cartridge files relevant to the current task
5. Inspect open decisions, promotion queue, and do-not-resurrect notes
6. Proceed with the smallest safe next action

## 15.2 Restart failure modes

The cartridge is not restart-safe if: current phase is unclear, `01_PROJECT.md` is stale, `README.md` is missing or out of date, active decisions exist only in chat, legacy artifacts remain ambiguous, or the next action cannot be inferred.

---

# 16. Migration Mechanics

## 16.1 Migration objective

Preserve useful responsibilities from older artifact systems without preserving obsolete structure.

## 16.2 Migration destination map

| Legacy Content Type | New Destination |
|---|---|
| Reusable collaboration rules | `HARNESS.md` |
| Current status and resume notes | `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` |
| Product scope and user intent | `.harness/02_PRD.md` |
| Technical structure and build mechanics | `.harness/03_BUILD.md` |
| Execution constraints | `.harness/03_BUILD.md` or optional execution artifact |
| Changelog or decision trail | `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` or optional changelog |
| Rejected paths worth remembering | `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` |
| Obsolete or duplicated content | Prune |

## 16.3 Migration validation

After migration: old artifact responsibilities have a destination, duplicated governance is removed, stale assumptions are pruned, durable decisions are promoted, and superseded artifacts are not ambiguously authoritative.

---

# 17. Drift Handling

Artifact drift occurs when one file changes but related files are not updated.

When drift is detected:

1. identify affected artifacts and the authority domain for the topic
2. decide which artifact controls
3. update the stale artifact
4. prune obsolete notes
5. record meaningful resolution in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`

Drift that prevents safe continuation — missing artifacts, conflicting authority, or active decisions existing only in chat — requires pausing large changes and resolving the conflict before proceeding. Cosmetic drift (minor wording mismatches, harmless formatting inconsistencies) can be fixed when convenient without blocking progress.

---

# 18. Artifact Acceptance Checklist

Before editing an artifact, confirm the scope is clear. After editing, confirm:

- file name and location are correct
- required sections are present and local document governance is intact
- no durable decisions were silently removed
- assumptions made during editing are visible in the conversation
- related artifacts requiring updates are identified
- old artifacts are not left ambiguously authoritative
- next action remains clear

Before declaring the cartridge stable, confirm:

- `01_PROJECT.md` and `README.md` exist and are current
- all cartridge files have clear roles and Document Governance sections
- open decisions are tracked and promotion queue is current
- no legacy artifact remains ambiguously authoritative

---

# 19. Acceptance Gates

The cartridge is stable when:

- `01_PROJECT.md` and `README.md` exist, are current, and accurately reflect the project
- each cartridge file has a clear role, local governance, and no material overlap with others
- edits are precise and do not silently remove durable decisions
- stable decisions are promoted and post-promotion pruning is done
- meaningful changes are captured in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` or a justified changelog
- a future session can resume from the files alone
- no legacy artifact remains ambiguously authoritative

---

# 20. Implementation Notes

Keep the system markdown-first. Do not assume tooling is required. The artifact model should work as a folder of markdown files.

Do not overfit to one project. This Build Spec governs the artifact operating system and should not absorb implementation details from a specific downstream product.

Prefer explicit section contracts over clever implicit structure — clear headings and required sections are easier for an AI model to follow.

Preserve optionality for future tooling: the current system should not require automation, but should be structured enough that future tools could validate file names, required sections, governance presence, unresolved decisions, and cross-file references.

Treat legacy files as source material unless promoted. They do not become current authority unless intentionally promoted into the canonical artifact set.

---

# 21. Document Governance

## 21.1 This file owns

Stable build mechanics: canonical file map, deployment topology, artifact contracts, authority mechanics, lifecycle mechanics, promotion and pruning mechanics, artifact update mechanics, editing discipline, changelog and versioning mechanics, optional artifact extension policy, validation rules, restart mechanics, migration mechanics, drift handling, and acceptance gates.

## 21.2 This file does not own

Current project status, active work, product rationale, or reusable collaboration protocol. Those belong in `.harness/01_PROJECT.md`, `.harness/02_PRD.md`, and `HARNESS.md` respectively.

## 21.3 Update this file when

The canonical file map changes, artifact contracts change, validation rules change, acceptance gates change, migration mechanics change, or optional artifact extension rules change.

## 21.4 Do not update this file when

Only the current project phase changes, a product goal changes without affecting build mechanics, or a decision is still unresolved.

## 21.5 Conflict behavior

For build mechanics, this file has authority over `.harness/01_PROJECT.md` and `.harness/02_PRD.md`. Defers to `HARNESS.md` for collaboration protocol, `.harness/02_PRD.md` for product intent, and direct operator instruction for the current task.

---

# 22. Final Summary

The Harness is a governing instrument built as a markdown artifact package. When applied to a host project, it deposits a `.harness/` knowledge cartridge at the project root, with `HARNESS.md` as the governing protocol.

The cartridge always contains `01_PROJECT.md` as its session anchor and `README.md` as its index. Everything else is composed by the operator from available templates or created as the project requires.

This Build Specification defines how the cartridge is structured, validated, maintained, migrated, extended, and stabilized. The system is stable only when artifacts are role-clear, locally governed, cross-consistent, restart-safe, and free of ambiguously authoritative legacy files.
